story:
An interviewer in thick-rimmed glasses sits with John in what looks like a hospital room. He offers water; John declines and tightens the blanket over his shoulders. John only wants to see his wife, Scarlett. The interviewer asks about the fire. John says he noticed it around 2:30 a.m. He was downstairs watching stock market news because he couldn’t sleep. Scarlett, he insists, was upstairs in bed.

Smoke, he says, poured in from the garage, thick enough to blind him in the kitchen. He didn’t call 911—he wanted to make sure “Scar” was safe. He went upstairs. The guest room above the garage was burning through the floor. He entered the bedroom and woke Scarlett. No fire there, he swears. They ran down the stairs and out the front door. The neighbor, Mrs. Jeannet Walker, had already called for help.

The interviewer keeps pressing the timeline, his pen scratching notes. Was John sure Scarlett had been asleep? Was there fire in the bedroom? When he came down the stairs, didn’t he notice the stairwell engulfed, the first floor consumed? John stiffens. He repeats himself: they ran out together. Ask Scarlett, he says. She’ll confirm it.

But the interviewer has a statement from Mrs. Walker. She never saw them come through the front door. She never saw Scarlett at all. The room tightens around John. He asks for his lawyer. He gets up to leave. An orderly named Donovan—big, solid, dressed like hospital staff—blocks the doorway. John sits again, shaking, the blanket sliding to the floor.

“Then you called the police?” the interviewer asks. “No.” “Why not?” “The neighbor already had.” The interviewer quietly steps closer to the truth. John tries to bolt. Donovan catches him and lowers him back into the chair, firm but controlled, making sure he doesn’t hurt himself. John demands to see his wife. He refuses to answer anything else.

The interviewer removes his glasses, rubs his eyes, and finally says it: “Your wife didn’t make it.” John rejects it. He says he saved her. The interviewer holds his gaze. “You only think you did. You had a psychotic break.” John fights the words, calls out for Scarlett, begs her to answer. The interviewer’s hands steady his head. “It’s not last night, John. It was two years ago. You tried to save her. You did what you could. You have to accept it.”

John collapses into him, sobbing. Donovan releases his hold. The interviewer signals, “End simulation four-three-oh-two.”

The hospital vanishes. White walls. White furniture. The two men stand revealed in all-white clothing, their skin painted to match the sterile set. The interview had been a controlled reconstruction, a therapeutic simulation meant to walk John to the truth he keeps refusing to face. The careful questions, the staged contradictions, the orderly’s restraint—tools to keep him safe and coax him toward acceptance without harm. The interviewer brushes off his pants and tells John they’ll try again. They will keep working, no matter how many times it takes.
